figures.caption: Figure 5: This is an example of verifiability beyond answer correctness. Both methods predict the correct answer, but differ in evidence quality. AKS misses the critical temporal transition (birds flying away), leading to an incomplete and partially inconsistent rationale. In contrast, CATS performs event-centric dense sampling, capturing the key transition and producing a coherent, visually grounded. This illustrates that correct answers alone do not guarantee faithful reasoning. See Appendix A.3 for full description.

Figure 1: Efficiency–performance trade-off of CATS compared to MIRA. CATS reduces preprocessing time by up to ∼31.6× and memory usage by ∼10.7×, while retaining 93–98% of MIRA’s accuracy. This demonstrates that effective frame selection can achieve near-equivalent performance at a fraction of the computational cost, offering a favorable point on the efficiency–accuracy frontier.

Figure 4: Illustration of the CATS selection process. Given a query and a video, frame-level relevance scores form a temporal relevance signal. At each iteration, the highest-scoring frame is selected and a suppression region is applied to nearby frames based on a radius determined by video length and budget. This suppression is adaptively modulated by curvature, resulting in smaller radii around sharp peaks and larger radii in flatter regions. As the selection proceeds, the influence of previously selected frames decays, allowing new informative regions to emerge. The final selected frames are passed to an MLLM for response generation.

Figure 2: Comparison of keyframe selection strategies. Given the same query, AKS selects frames based on a relevance–coverage trade-off and fails to capture the critical moment, leading to an incorrect prediction. In contrast, CATS leverages temporal curvature to identify regions of high semantic variation, selecting frames concentrated around the key event, thereby enabling the MLLM to produce the correct answer.
Table 1: Overall video question answering accuracy (%) on LongVideoBench (LVB) validation set and VideoMME (V-MME).

Figure 6: From rationale fidelity to future-query reasoning. Although both methods produce the correct answer, AKS introduces hallucinated details due to missing visual evidence, resulting in inconsistent explanations and failure on follow-up questions. In contrast, CATS preserves critical temporal and contextual information, enabling more consistent and evidence-aligned rationales and accurate responses to future queries. This demonstrates that grounded frame selection directly impacts downstream reasoning reliability. See Appendix A.3 for full description.

Figure 3: Overview of the proposed CATS framework. Given a user query and a video, frame-level relevance scores are computed using a vision-language model, forming a relevance signal over time. CATS iteratively selects frames by identifying high-relevance candidates and applying an adaptive suppression mechanism modulated by temporal curvature, which controls redundancy while preserving both short, high-salience events and extended informative regions. The selected keyframes are then provided to an MLLM to generate the final response.
